insights: The five managers in the IT department are assigned system user administration tasks with perfect uniformity—each holds exactly 20% of assignments, indicating no individual is disproportionately burdened.

The five assignment groups handling system user administration workloads are distributed with perfect uniformity—each group manages exactly 20% of cases, showing no structural concentration of responsibility.

System user administration updates are nearly equally attributed to 'admin', 'employee', and 'system' actors (33.4%, 33.4%, and 33.2% respectively), confirming no single administrative actor type dominates update activity or bears excessive operational load.

No dimension—individual assignee, functional group, or update actor type—exhibits concentration above 33.4%, ruling out statistically meaningful imbalances that could signal management overload or governance risk.

The absence of role or group concentration across all three validated dimensions suggests current delegation practices in system user administration are operationally balanced and sustainable without immediate reallocation.
summary: The evaluation finds no evidence of managerial role imbalance in IT system user administration that would indicate management overload: assigned_to, assignment_group, and sys_updated_by all show uniform or near-uniform distributions, with maximum shares capped at 33.4%. This confirms equitable workload distribution across individuals, teams, and actor types—no single point of pressure, bottleneck, or governance risk is present. The current operational model appears stable and well-delegated; ongoing monitoring of these distributions remains advisable to detect early deviations as scale or process changes occur.
